key_insight: Electron beam welding can successfully reconstitute Charpy impact specimens for reactor-pressure-vessel steels, producing dense weld zones with no obvious cracking and near-original mechanical behavior in the best case.
engineering_relevance: This supports specimen reconstitution for nuclear materials testing, helping preserve safety-relevant mechanical evaluation when original material is limited or unavailable.
method: Reconstituted Charpy impact specimens of 16MND5, 10Cr12Ni3Mo2VN, and X6NiCrTiMoVB 25-15-2 steels using electron beam welding.
Characterized weld-zone microstructure with optical microscopy and scanning electron microscopy.
Assessed hardness, impact toughness, and flaw presence to compare restructured specimens with original ones.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports non-destructive flaw detection plus microscopy and mechanical tests, showing a dense weld zone without obvious cracks and the closest property match for 10Cr12Ni3Mo2VN.
watch_item: Results are based on specific steel grades and specimen-scale reconstitution, so broader process transfer to other alloys or larger components may require additional validation.
paper_title: Effects of electron beam welding on the microstructure and impact properties of three reconstituted steels
